The person concluded the session by deciding to gather more expertise before building, specifically around LSP and TreeSitter internals, real-world CLAUDE.md usage patterns, and MCP server fundamentals. The person demonstrated a preference for iterative conceptual refinement before implementation, and showed interest in building Neo potentially as a standalone CLI tool or MCP extension. Domain-specific terminology used throughout includes: context window, tokens, CLAUDE.md, MCP, LSP, TreeSitter, ctags, monorepo, system prompt, and incremental indexing.

Good, I have enough info to give a solid answer.Yes, absolutely. Claude's context window holds your entire conversation — every message, every file Claude reads, and every command output. A single debugging session or codebase exploration can generate tens of thousands of tokens.

Here's what's actually happening and how to deal with it:

**Why it greps instead of indexing**

Claude Code doesn't pre-index your codebase. Instead, it uses search tools to navigate your codebase on the fly as needed. The problem is that on large repos, iterative grep exploration burns context faster than it narrows — especially for common terms that return hundreds of matches across dozens of files.

**Practical mitigations:**

1. **Use a `CLAUDE.md` file** — Run `/init` to generate a starter `CLAUDE.md` that Claude reads at the start of every conversation. This gives it architectural context upfront so it doesn't need to explore as much.

2. **Monitor context usage** — Run `/context` mid-session to understand how you're using your 200k token window. A fresh session in a monorepo can cost a baseline ~20k tokens (10%) just to start.

3. **Semantic search via MCP** — Tools like [Claude Context](https://github.com/zilliztech/claude-context) add vector-based semantic search. In testing, token usage dropped over 40% without loss in recall compared to grep-based retrieval.

4. **Be specific in prompts** — Instead of vague tasks that force exploration, point Claude directly to relevant files upfront: *"Look at `src/auth/token.ts` and fix the refresh logic"* vs *"fix auth"*.

5. **Start fresh sessions for new tasks** — Don't let a session balloon with unrelated context before switching tasks.

The grep behavior is a known pain point — there's even an open GitHub issue requesting proper codebase indexing built into Claude Code.

content.title: Why I’m Against Claude Code’s Grep-Only Retrieval? It Just Burns Too Many Tokens - Milvus Blog
content.text:
In side-by-side testing: Token usage dropped by over 40%, without any loss in recall. That translates directly into lower API costs and faster responses. Alternatively, with the same budget, Claude Context delivered far more accurate retrievals.

Claude Code is a strong tool—but it has poor code context. Cursor solved this with codebase indexing, but Cursor is closed-source, locked behind subscriptions, and pricey for individuals or small teams. That gap is why we started building our own open-source solution: Claude Context. Claude Context is an open-source MCP plugin that brings semantic code search to Claude Code (and any other AI coding agent that speaks MCP). Instead of brute-forcing your repo with grep, it integrates vector databases with embedding models to give LLMs deep, targeted context from your entire codebase. The result: sharper retrieval, less token waste, and a far better developer experience. ... We wanted this to work everywhere—not just Claude. MCP (Model Context Protocol) acts like the USB standard for LLMs, letting external tools plug in seamlessly. By packaging Claude Context as an MCP server, it works not only with Claude Code but also with Gemini CLI, Qwen Code, Cline, and even Cursor.

Once my codebase was indexed, I gave Claude Code the same bug description that had previously sent it on a five-minute grep-powered goose chase. This time, through claude-context MCP calls, it immediately pinpointed the exact file and line number, complete with an explanation of the issue. The difference wasn’t subtle—it was night and day. And it wasn’t just bug hunting. With Claude Context integrated, Claude Code consistently produced higher-quality results across: ... The performance boost shows up in the numbers, too. In side-by-side testing: Token usage dropped by over 40%, without any loss in recall. That translates directly into lower API costs and faster responses. Alternatively, with the same budget, Claude Context delivered far more accurate retrievals. We have open-sourced Claude Context on GitHub, and it has earned 2.6K+ stars already.

All synchronization state persists locally in the user’s ~/.context/merkle/ directory. Each codebase maintains its own independent snapshot file containing file hash tables and serialized Merkle tree data, ensuring accurate state recovery even after program restarts. This design delivers obvious benefits: most checks complete in milliseconds when no changes exist, only genuinely modified files trigger reprocessing (avoiding massive computational waste), and state recovery works flawlessly across program sessions. From a user experience perspective, modifying a single function triggers re-indexing for only that file, not the entire project, dramatically improving development efficiency. Even the smartest indexing engine is useless without a clean developer-facing interface. MCP was the obvious choice, but it introduced unique challenges: The MCP module serves as the user-facing interface, making user experience the top priority.

Vadim Nicolai's blog on software engineering, AI, web development, and technology insights.

Staleness: an index built at session start is stale as soon as the first file changes. Reliability: every additional system is a failure point; vector DBs have latency spikes, embedding APIs have rate limits, sync pipelines have bugs. The strongest published critique of agentic search came from Milvus. Their argument: "Grep is a dead end that drowns you in irrelevant matches, burns tokens, and stalls your workflow. Without semantic understanding, it's like asking your AI to debug blindfolded." They propose their Claude Context vector MCP plugin as a hybrid fix, claiming 40% token reduction. Milvus sells a vector database. That commercial interest is transparent and worth noting. It does not make the technical criticism wrong. The token burn problem is real on common terms. Search useState across a React codebase and you will get hundreds of matches across dozens of files.

Claude Code must either process all of them (expensive) or refine the query (adds turns). On codebases with inconsistent naming or high churn, the refinement loop can consume substantial context before reaching the target file. The 200K token pricing cliff makes this worse when hit: any request exceeding 200K input tokens pays 2x on all tokens in that request, not just the excess. The jump from ... 3/million to 3/millionto6/million is a hard threshold, not a gradual escalation — and agentic sessions on large codebases with vague prompts can hit it faster than expected. The semantic miss problem is the other genuine limitation. Grep finds what you name. If createD1HttpClient was renamed buildGatewayClient six months ago, grep finds nothing. Vector embeddings preserve semantic relationships across renames — a real advantage on codebases with heavy refactoring history or cryptic abbreviation conventions.

In practice, Claude Code compensates by running multiple searches: "auth", "session", "token", "middleware", "jwt", "bearer" — triangulating toward the module rather than naming it directly. This multi-step reasoning is something static embedding retrieval cannot do (a vector DB returns its top-k hits and stops). But it costs more turns and more tokens than a single well-placed semantic query would. ... Well-named, medium-sized codebases — consistent naming discipline eliminates most semantic miss risk ... Large monorepos — millions of lines where iterative grep exploration burns context faster than it narrows · Conceptual search — "find all places we handle authentication" without knowing exact symbol names · Unfamiliar codebases — when you cannot yet name what you are looking for, semantic similarity is more useful than exact match ·

content.text:
The Takeaway: Treat your CLAUDE.md as a high-level, curated set of guardrails and pointers. Use it to guide where you need to invest in more AI (and human) friendly tools, rather than trying to make it a comprehensive manual. Thanks for reading Shrivu’s Substack! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work. I recommend running /context mid coding session at least once to understand how you are using your 200k token context window (even with Sonnet-1M, I don’t trust that the full context window is actually used effectively). For us a fresh session in our monorepo costs a baseline ~20k tokens (10%) with the remaining 180k for making your change — which can fill up quite fast. A screenshot of /context in one of my recent side projects. You can almost think of this like disk space that fills up as you work on a feature. After a few minutes or hours you’ll need to clear the messages (purple) to make space to continue.

The single most important file in your codebase for using Claude Code effectively is the root CLAUDE.md. This file is the agent’s “constitution,” its primary source of truth for how your specific repository works. How you treat this file depends on the context. For my hobby projects, I let Claude dump whatever it wants in there. For my professional work, our monorepo’s CLAUDE.md is strictly maintained and currently sits at 13KB (I could easily see it growing to 25KB). It only documents tools and APIs used by 30% (arbitrary) or more of our engineers (else tools are documented in product or library specific markdown files) We’ve even started allocating effectively a max token count for each internal tool’s documentation, almost like selling “ad space” to teams. If you can’t explain your tool concisely, it’s not ready for the CLAUDE.md.

After a few minutes or hours you’ll need to clear the messages (purple) to make space to continue.

On paper, custom subagents are Claude Code’s most powerful feature for context management. The pitch is simple: a complex task requires X tokens of input context (e.g., how to run tests), accumulates Y tokens of working context, and produces a Z token answer. Running N tasks means (X + Y + Z) * N tokens in your main window. The subagent solution is to farm out the (X + Y) * N work to specialized agents, which only return the final Z token answers, keeping your main context clean. I find they are a powerful idea that, in practice, custom subagents create two new problems: They Gatekeep Context: If I make a PythonTests subagent, I’ve now hidden all testing context from my main agent. It can no longer reason holistically about a change. It’s now forced to invoke the subagent just to know how to validate its own code. They Force Human Workflows: Worse, they force Claude into a rigid, human-defined workflow.

On a simple level, I use claude --resume and claude --continue frequently. They’re great for restarting a bugged terminal or quickly rebooting an older session. I’ll often claude --resume a session from days ago just to ask the agent to summarize how it overcame a specific error, which I then use to improve our CLAUDE.md and internal tooling. More in the weeds, Claude Code stores all session history in ~/.claude/projects/ to tap into the raw historical session data. I have scripts that run meta-analysis on these logs, looking for common exceptions, permission requests, and error patterns to help improve agent-facing context. The Takeaway: Use claude --resume and claude --continue to restart sessions and uncover buried historical context. Hooks are huge. I don’t use them for hobby projects, but they are critical for steering Claude in a complex enterprise repo.

async function updateMetrics() { let allMessages = []; // Step 1: Collect ALL messages from ALL projects for (const basePath of claudeDataPaths) { const projectDirs = fs.readdirSync(basePath); for (const projectDir of projectDirs) { const projectPath = path.join(basePath, projectDir); const files = fs.readdirSync(projectPath).filter(f => f.endsWith('.jsonl')); for (const file of files) { const filePath = path.join(projectPath, file); const messages = await parseSessionFile(filePath); // Collect ALL messages (we’ll dedupe and filter later) allMessages.push(...messages); } } } // Step 2: Deduplicate by message ID (messages can appear in multiple files) const uniqueMessages = Array.from( new Map(allMessages.map(m => [m.id, m])).values() ); // Step 3: Calculate metrics for the active session const metrics = calculateSessionMetrics(uniqueMessages, 'global'); if (metrics && metrics.isActive) { statusBar.update(metrics, planConfig); } } // Update immediately on activation updateMetrics(); // 

User-specific tracking — Sessions aren’t project-specific, they’re user-specific, so we check ALL files and pick the most recently active one · Claude Code uses 5-hour rolling sessions.

I initially tried using `chokidar` for file watching, but it was unreliable — events wouldn’t trigger consistently, especially on startup. The extension showed “No active session” even when Claude Code was actively running. File watchers in VS Code extensions face several issues: Initial scan problems— `ignoreInitial: false` didn’t always trigger · Event timing — Files might update before the watcher is ready · Complexity — Managing watchers, handlers, and cleanup is error-prone ·

Claude Code stores conversation data locally in JSONL (JSON Lines) files, but finding and parsing them isn’t straightforward. The files are scattered across project directories with unpredictable names and nested data structures. ... Each project gets its own directory (named after the workspace path), and each session is a UUID-based file like `c26cf239-e94b-4bf8-bfad-9e36872caf2d.jsonl`. ... function getClaudeDataPaths(): string[] { const homeDir = os.homedir(); return [ path.join(homeDir, '.config', 'claude', 'projects'), path.join(homeDir, '.claude', 'projects'), ].filter(p => fs.existsSync(p)); } Each line in the file is a separate JSON object representing one message in the conversation. But here’s the tricky part — the structure is **nested**: { "type": "assistant", "timestamp": "2025-10-04T11:24:54.135Z", "sessionId": "c26cf239-…", "message": { "id": "msg_01…", "role": "assistant", "usage": { "input_tokens": 7, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 464, "cache_read_input_tokens": 3768

async function updateMetrics() { let allMessages = []; // Step 1: Collect ALL messages from ALL projects for (const basePath of claudeDataPaths) { const projectDirs = fs.readdirSync(basePath); for (const projectDir of projectDirs) { const projectPath = path.join(basePath, projectDir); const files = fs.readdirSync(projectPath).filter(f => f.endsWith('.jsonl')); for (const file of files) { const filePath = path.join(projectPath, file); const messages = await parseSessionFile(filePath); // Collect ALL messages (we’ll dedupe and filter later) allMessages.push(...messages); } } } // Step 2: Deduplicate by message ID (messages can appear in multiple files) const uniqueMessages = Array.from( new Map(allMessages.map(m => [m.id, m])).values() ); // Step 3: Calculate metrics for the active session const metrics = calculateSessionMetrics(uniqueMessages, 'global'); if (metrics && metrics.isActive) { statusBar.update(metrics, planConfig); } } // Update immediately on activation updateMetrics(); //

Extension for Visual Studio Code - Real-time monitoring of Claude Code token usage, cost tracking, and session management

Visual Studio Code>Other>Claude Code Usage TrackerNew to Visual Studio Code?

content.thinking: Good, I have enough info to give a solid answer.

summaries.summary: Assembled sufficient knowledge to address token consumption query.

content.text:
Yes, absolutely. Claude's context window holds your entire conversation — every message, every file Claude reads, and every command output. A single debugging session or codebase exploration can generate tens of thousands of tokens.

Here's what's actually happening and how to deal with it:

**Why it greps instead of indexing**

Claude Code doesn't pre-index your codebase. Instead, it uses search tools to navigate your codebase on the fly as needed. The problem is that on large repos, iterative grep exploration burns context faster than it narrows — especially for common terms that return hundreds of matches across dozens of files.

**Practical mitigations:**

1. **Use a `CLAUDE.md` file** — Run `/init` to generate a starter `CLAUDE.md` that Claude reads at the start of every conversation. This gives it architectural context upfront so it doesn't need to explore as much.

2. **Monitor context usage** — Run `/context` mid-session to understand how you're using your 200k token window. A fresh session in a monorepo can cost a baseline ~20k tokens (10%) just to start.

3. **Semantic search via MCP** — Tools like [Claude Context](https://github.com/zilliztech/claude-context) add vector-based semantic search. In testing, token usage dropped over 40% without loss in recall compared to grep-based retrieval.

4. **Be specific in prompts** — Instead of vague tasks that force exploration, point Claude directly to relevant files upfront: *"Look at `src/auth/token.ts` and fix the refresh logic"* vs *"fix auth"*.

5. **Start fresh sessions for new tasks** — Don't let a session balloon with unrelated context before switching tasks.

The grep behavior is a known pain point — there's even an open GitHub issue requesting proper codebase indexing built into Claude Code.

chat_messages.text:
The user is essentially describing a CLAUDE.md file or a similar architecture document that gives Claude Code upfront context about the project structure, so it doesn't need to grep around. This is exactly what CLAUDE.md is for. Let me give a thoughtful response.You're basically describing `CLAUDE.md` — and yes, it's one of the best ways to reduce that exploratory grepping.

The idea is sound: if Claude already knows *where things live*, it skips the treasure hunt and goes straight to the right file.

**What makes a good architecture file for this:**

- **Directory map with purpose** — not just the tree, but *what each folder does*. e.g. `src/services/` → business logic, `src/api/` → route handlers
- **Key entry points** — "auth starts in `src/middleware/auth.ts`", "DB config is `src/config/db.ts`"
- **Naming conventions** — so Claude can *guess* the filename before searching
- **What NOT to touch** — generated files, vendor code, etc.

**The difference between a bad and good CLAUDE.md:**

```
# Bad — Claude still has to explore
"This is a Next.js app with a Postgres database"

# Good — Claude can navigate directly
"Auth: src/lib/auth/ (JWT in token.ts, session in session.ts)
DB models: src/models/ (User.ts, Order.ts, Product.ts)
API routes: src/app/api/ — follow Next.js App Router conventions
Shared utils: src/lib/utils.ts"
```

**One practical tip:** keep it *lean*. A bloated CLAUDE.md ironically wastes tokens on every session load. Think of it as a map, not documentation — just enough to navigate, not explain.

The real win is that Claude stops issuing 5 grep calls to find the auth module and just opens the right file immediately. That alone can save thousands of tokens per session on a medium-sized codebase.

Have you already got a `CLAUDE.md` in your project, or are you starting fresh?

chat_messages.text:
The user is asking whether maintaining a CLAUDE.md or similar architecture guide file is an industry-standard practice. Let me answer from what I know - this is more of an emerging practice than a universal standard.Honestly? **Not yet.** It's more of an emerging best practice among power users, not something the industry has standardized on.

**The reality right now:**

- Most teams are still figuring out AI-assisted workflows on the fly
- Many developers don't even know `CLAUDE.md` exists
- Those who *do* use it have wildly inconsistent approaches — some write novels, some write 5 lines

**What actually exists as "standards":**

The closest thing to industry consensus is keeping *some* form of AI context file, but it goes by different names depending on the tool:
- `CLAUDE.md` → Claude Code
- `.cursorrules` / `cursor.mdc` → Cursor  
- `AGENTS.md` → OpenAI Codex / general agents
- `Copilot Instructions` → GitHub Copilot

Each tool has its own convention. There's no cross-tool standard yet.

**The deeper problem:**

Most codebases weren't *designed* with AI agents in mind. So the architecture knowledge exists only in senior devs' heads, scattered READMEs, or Confluence pages nobody reads. Translating that into a tight, token-efficient agent context file is genuinely new work.

**Where it's heading:**

Teams that are serious about AI-assisted development are starting to treat `CLAUDE.md` (or equivalent) as a first-class artifact — maintained alongside the codebase, reviewed in PRs. That's probably where the industry lands eventually.

But right now? You'd be ahead of most teams just by having a well-structured one.

chat_messages.text:
The user is describing essentially a lightweight codebase index/map that an AI agent creates once and references repeatedly. Let me give an honest opinion.Honest opinion: **the core idea is solid, the hard problems are in the details.**

**What's genuinely good about it:**
- One-time scan cost vs. repeated grep costs — good economics
- A compact representation decouples "understanding the codebase" from "consuming tokens every session"
- Tool-agnostic by design — any agent can read a file

**The hard problems you'll need to solve:**

**1. Staleness**
Code changes constantly. Neo becomes a lie the moment someone renames a file or refactors a module. You need a strategy — file watchers, git hooks, or a "re-scan on drift" trigger.

**2. What do you actually put in it?**
This is the core design challenge. Too much → defeats the purpose. Too little → agent still greps. The right abstraction level is genuinely hard:
- File paths? Easy but shallow
- Function signatures? More useful but bloats fast
- Semantic summaries? Powerful but expensive to generate

**3. It already exists in various forms**
- `ctags` / `etags` — old school symbol indexes
- LSP (Language Server Protocol) — does this at IDE level
- TreeSitter — AST-based code understanding
- The MCP tools mentioned earlier (Claude Context, etc.)

The gap is that none of these are *AI-native* — designed specifically to be consumed efficiently by an LLM.

**Where your idea could be genuinely differentiated:**

If Neo is:
- Generated *by* an AI (so it captures semantic meaning, not just symbols)
- Structured specifically for *LLM consumption* (not human reading)
- Incrementally updated (not full rescans)
- Small enough to fit in a system prompt

...that's actually a meaningful product gap that doesn't exist cleanly today.

**Biggest risk:**
You're essentially building a cache. Caches are simple in theory, brutal in engineering. The invalidation problem alone is a rabbit hole.

What's your instinct on the format — leaning towards structured data (JSON/YAML) or something more narrative?

chat_messages.text:
The user wants to fully idealize the "Neo" concept. Let me think deeply about this and give comprehensive, thoughtful ideas.Good — incremental updates by the same agent that makes changes is the right call. That solves the staleness problem cleanly.

Now let's fully idealize it.

---

**What Neo should contain:**

**Layer 1 — Structure** (skeleton)
```
src/auth/ → handles all authentication
  token.ts → JWT creation, validation, refresh
  session.ts → session store, expiry logic
```
Not just paths — *intent* of each file in one line.

**Layer 2 — Relationships** (the graph)
- What imports what
- Which modules are shared vs isolated
- Which files are "high traffic" (touched by many other files)

This is what grep can *never* tell you. Knowing `utils.ts` is imported by 40 files changes how carefully the agent edits it.

**Layer 3 — Conventions** (the rules)
```
error handling → always throw AppError class
API responses → use sendResponse() helper
env vars → accessed only through config/env.ts, never process.env directly
```
Agent learns the codebase's "style" without reading every file.

**Layer 4 — Change history** (lightweight)
```
last modified: auth/token.ts, api/user.ts
last added: services/payment.ts
```
Helps agent understand where active development is happening.

---

**Neo's format — my recommendation:**

Not JSON. Not YAML. **A hybrid.**

```
[STRUCTURE]
src/auth/token.ts | JWT logic | imports: config/env, lib/errors
src/auth/session.ts | session management | imports: models/User

[CONVENTIONS]
error-handling: AppError class
db-access: only through src/models/*
env: config/env.ts only

[HOTSPOTS]
src/utils/api.ts | imported-by: 34 files | edit carefully
src/config/db.ts | core dependency | never delete

[RECENT]
modified: src/services/payment.ts, src/api/checkout.ts
```

Human-readable, LLM-friendly, trivially parseable. No bloat from XML/JSON syntax overhead.

---

**How the agent maintains it:**

```
after every file creation   → add entry to [STRUCTURE]
after every file deletion   → remove entry
after every refactor        → update imports map
after adding a convention   → agent asks "should I log this in Neo?"
periodically                → re-rank [HOTSPOTS] by import count
```

The key insight: **the agent writes Neo in the same commit as the code change.** They're atomic.

---

**Neo's size target:**

Should fit in **~2,000–4,000 tokens** regardless of codebase size. That's your hard constraint. If it grows beyond that, Neo needs summarization — not more detail.

Think of it like a city map vs a satellite image. A map is more useful precisely *because* it throws information away.

---

**Advanced ideas:**

- **Neo diff** — agent only reads the *delta* of Neo since last session, not the whole thing
- **Neo confidence scores** — mark entries as `[stale?]` if a file was modified but Neo wasn't updated
- **Scoped Neo** — for monorepos, each package has its own Neo, root Neo just maps packages
- **Neo as MCP tool** — instead of injecting into context, agent queries Neo on demand: `neo.query("where is auth?")` — returns only relevant slice

---

**Honest gap to solve:**

The first Neo generation is expensive — full codebase scan. You need to make this a one-time setup that developers *want* to run. Think:

```bash
npx neo init
```

Scans, generates, commits `neo.md` to the repo. From there the agent owns it.

---

**What makes Neo genuinely novel vs existing tools:**

| | ctags | LSP | CLAUDE.md | Neo |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AI-native format | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Semantic intent | ❌ | ❌ | partial | ✅ |
| Incremental | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Token-optimized | ❌ | ❌ | partial | ✅ |
| Agent-maintained | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |

That last row is the real moat. **No existing tool is maintained by the agent itself.**

What's your instinct — build Neo as a standalone CLI tool, or as a Claude Code extension via MCP?

The user is asking about multi-contributor scenarios for Neo. This is essentially a distributed systems / merge conflict problem. Let me think through this carefully.This is essentially a **distributed state problem**. Same category of problems as git itself had to solve.

**The core tension:**

```
Dev A adds src/payments/stripe.ts → updates Neo locally
Dev B adds src/payments/paypal.ts → updates Neo locally
Both push → Neo has a merge conflict
```

But unlike code conflicts, Neo conflicts are *semantic* — two valid descriptions of the same codebase.

---

**Three directions to think in:**

**Direction 1 — Treat Neo like code (git-native)**

Neo is just a file in the repo. Merge conflicts are resolved like any other file. Simple, no infra needed.

Problem: merge conflicts in Neo will be frequent and annoying. Developers will start ignoring Neo maintenance. It dies quietly.

---

**Direction 2 — Neo is generated, not authored**

Nobody owns Neo. It's a build artifact — like a lockfile.

```bash
# runs in CI, or as a git hook
neo generate → overwrites neo.md from source of truth
```

No merge conflicts because humans never manually edit it. The *codebase* is the source of truth, Neo is derived from it.

This is probably the **most robust direction.**

---

**Direction 3 — Neo is a database, not a file**

Each contributor's changes are Neo *patches*, merged server-side.

```
Dev A pushes → "added: src/payments/stripe.ts | Stripe integration"
Dev B pushes → "added: src/payments/paypal.ts | PayPal integration"
Server merges both patches cleanly → single Neo
```

Like CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) — designed so patches never conflict by definition.

Powerful but complex. Overkill unless Neo becomes a hosted product.

---

**My honest recommendation:**

Combine Direction 1 and 2:

- Neo lives in the repo (Direction 1) — visible, versionable, diffable in PRs
- But it's **regeneratable on demand** (Direction 2) — so if it gets messy, `neo generate` fixes it
- Agent updates Neo incrementally during development
- CI validates Neo is in sync — fails PR if Neo is stale

```
local dev    → agent maintains Neo incrementally
PR merge     → CI checks Neo is accurate
If drifted   → auto-regenerate in CI, commit back
```

---

**The deeper question to answer:**

Who is the *source of truth* — the Neo file, or the codebase itself?

If Neo = derived artifact → generation approach wins
If Neo = living document with human/agent annotations → git-native approach wins

The answer changes your entire architecture. What's your instinct?

chat_messages.text:
Perfect. That's the right call and it simplifies everything.

**If codebase = source of truth, then Neo is a derived artifact.**

This means:

```
Codebase → [Neo Generator] → neo.md
```

Never the other way around. Neo is always regeneratable. If it gets corrupted, stale, or deleted — just regenerate. No data loss ever.

---

**This decision resolves the multi-contributor problem cleanly:**

```
Dev A merges → CI regenerates Neo → commits updated Neo
Dev B merges → CI regenerates Neo → commits updated Neo
```

No conflicts. No ownership debates. No stale entries. Neo is always a pure reflection of the current codebase state.

---

**Now the real engineering question becomes:**

> *How do you generate Neo efficiently enough that it stays accurate without being expensive?*

Two modes needed:

**Full generation** — run once, or when Neo drifts badly
```bash
neo init        # scans entire codebase, builds Neo from scratch
```

**Incremental generation** — triggered by file changes
```bash
neo update src/payments/stripe.ts    # only re-processes changed file
```

The incremental mode is what keeps it cheap day-to-day.

---

**What the generator needs to extract per file:**

```
filename | purpose (1 line) | exports | imports | complexity signal
```

That's it. Nothing more. The generator should be almost *ruthlessly minimal* — because every byte in Neo costs tokens in every future session.

---

**The generation pipeline:**

```
file changed
    ↓
parse imports/exports (TreeSitter or LSP)
    ↓
AI summarizes purpose in 1 line
    ↓
update Neo entry for that file
    ↓
recalculate hotspots (import frequency)
    ↓
write to neo.md
```

The only AI call is the one-line summary. Everything else is static analysis — fast and free.

---

**Rough token math:**

```
Average codebase: ~200 files
Per file in Neo:  ~20 tokens
Total Neo size:   ~4,000 tokens

Without Neo: agent greps 10-15 files × ~500 tokens each = 5,000-7,500 tokens per task
With Neo:    agent reads Neo once = 4,000 tokens, goes directly to right file

Savings per session: significant, compounds across every task
```

---

**What to build first:**

```
1. neo init    — full scan, generates neo.md
2. neo update  — incremental, triggered by agent after file change
3. CI hook     — validates Neo is in sync on every PR
```

That's your MVP. No server, no database, no MCP yet — just a CLI and a file.

Does this architecture feel right to you? Want to go deeper on the generator design or the neo.md format next?

chat_messages.text: instead of neo being a md file, can we think of alternative file type or data structure which takes less memory ( means saving tokens) and scan completes fast.

content.text: instead of neo being a md file, can we think of alternative file type or data structure which takes less memory ( means saving tokens) and scan completes fast.

chat_messages.text:
Great question. Let's think through this systematically.

**The two goals pulling in opposite directions:**
```
LLM needs to READ it     → human-readable, natural language friendly
Storage needs to be SMALL → binary, compressed, encoded
```

The sweet spot is somewhere between.

---

**Options ranked by token efficiency:**

**1. MessagePack (binary JSON)**
```
JSON:        {"file": "src/auth/token.ts", "purpose": "JWT logic"}  → 55 chars
MessagePack: binary equivalent                                        → ~30 bytes
```
- Fast to parse
- Not human readable
- LLM can't read it directly — needs a tool to query it

**2. SQLite**
- Single binary file
- Agent queries via `neo.query("where is auth?")`
- Extremely fast lookups
- Zero token cost until queried
- LLM never reads the whole thing — only gets back what it asks for

**3. Flat binary index (custom)**
```
[hash][path_offset][summary_offset][import_count]
```
- Fastest possible scan
- But you're essentially reinventing SQLite

**4. CBOR (Concise Binary Object Representation)**
- Like MessagePack but with a proper standard
- Smaller than JSON, faster than XML/YAML
- Still needs a tool layer for LLM access

---

**Honestly the most interesting direction:**

**SQLite + MCP tool**

```
Agent doesn't read Neo at all.
Agent queries Neo like a database.

agent: "find auth related files"
neo.query() → returns 3 rows → ~200 tokens

vs

agent reads entire neo.md → 4,000 tokens
```

This flips the model entirely. Instead of injecting Neo into context upfront, **Neo becomes an on-demand lookup tool.** Agent only pays token cost for what it actually needs.

---

**Token cost comparison:**

```
neo.md (markdown)     → 4,000 tokens, loaded every session
neo.md (YAML)         → 3,200 tokens, loaded every session  
neo.db (SQLite + MCP) → 0 tokens upfront, ~100-300 per query
```

SQLite wins by a massive margin if you add a query interface.

---

**The tradeoff to be aware of:**

| Format | Token cost | Scan speed | LLM readable | Query flexibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Markdown | high | fast | ✅ | ❌ |
| YAML/TOML | medium | fast | ✅ | ❌ |
| JSON | medium | fast | partial | ❌ |
| SQLite | near zero | very fast | ❌ | ✅ |
| MessagePack | low | very fast | ❌ | partial |

---

**My recommendation:**

**SQLite as the storage engine, with two interfaces:**

```
neo.db           → actual storage (binary, never touched by LLM directly)
neo summary      → tiny human-readable snapshot (~500 tokens, high level only)
neo.query(term)  → MCP tool, returns targeted slice on demand
```

The summary gives the agent orientation. The query tool gives it precision. The database keeps everything fast and small.

---

**This also solves your multi-contributor problem even more cleanly:**

```
SQLite supports concurrent reads
Writes serialized via CI
No merge conflicts ever — binary file, always regenerated
```

---

Does the SQLite + MCP query approach resonate? Because if it does, the next design decision is what the **schema** looks like — and that's where Neo gets really interesting.

Let me design a neo.md format specifically for a React Native TypeScript codebase.Perfect. Let's design neo.md specifically for a React Native TypeScript codebase.

---

**React Native specific structure to capture:**

```
screens/        → navigation targets
components/     → reusable UI
hooks/          → custom hooks
services/       → API calls, business logic
store/          → state management (Redux/Zustand/etc)
navigation/     → stack, tab, drawer configs
utils/          → helpers
types/          → TypeScript interfaces, types
assets/         → fonts, images (usually skip these)
```

---

**Proposed neo.md format:**

```markdown
# NEO
generated: 2024-01-15 | files: 87 | version: 1

## STRUCTURE
src/screens/HomeScreen.tsx | home feed, shows posts list | uses: PostCard, useFeed
src/screens/ProfileScreen.tsx | user profile view | uses: Avatar, useProfile
src/components/PostCard.tsx | reusable post UI component | uses: Typography, theme
src/hooks/useFeed.ts | fetches and paginates feed data | uses: api/feed, store/feed
src/services/api/feed.ts | feed API calls | uses: httpClient
src/store/feedSlice.ts | feed redux slice | uses: types/Feed
src/types/Feed.ts | Feed, Post, Comment interfaces
src/navigation/RootNavigator.tsx | root stack config | all screen entries here
src/utils/httpClient.ts | axios instance, interceptors | HOTSPOT: 34 imports

## CONVENTIONS
state: Redux Toolkit (store/*)
api-calls: only through services/api/* never direct fetch
navigation: useNavigation hook, never prop drilling
env-vars: config/env.ts only
types: all shared types in src/types/*
styling: StyleSheet.create only, no inline styles
error-handling: AppError class in utils/errors.ts

## HOTSPOTS
src/utils/httpClient.ts | 34 imports | edit carefully
src/types/index.ts | 28 imports | edit carefully
src/theme/index.ts | 41 imports | edit carefully

## ENTRY POINTS
navigation: src/navigation/RootNavigator.tsx
app entry: index.ts → App.tsx
env config: src/config/env.ts
api base: src/services/api/httpClient.ts
```

---

**What each section saves:**

- `STRUCTURE` → agent goes directly to file, no grep
- `CONVENTIONS` → agent doesn't read 5 files to guess the pattern
- `HOTSPOTS` → agent knows what to be careful with
- `ENTRY POINTS` → agent orientates instantly on session start

---

**What to explicitly leave out:**

```
❌ node_modules
❌ assets/ (images, fonts)
❌ *.test.ts files (unless specifically relevant)
❌ config files (babel.config.js, metro.config.js)
❌ generated files (*.generated.ts)
```

These add tokens with near zero navigation value.

---

**Next step — the generator:**

You need a script that:

```
1. walks src/ directory
2. for each .ts / .tsx file:
   - extracts imports (static analysis)
   - counts how many files import it (hotspot score)
   - asks AI for one-line purpose summary
3. detects conventions from existing patterns
4. writes neo.md to root
```

The user is asking about token consumption for running `neo init` - the initial scan of the codebase to generate neo.md. Let me think through this carefully.

For neo init, the AI needs to:
1. Read each file
2. Generate a one-line summary for each file

Let me estimate the token costs.Great question to ask *before* building. Let's do the math.

---

**What neo init does that costs tokens:**

The only AI call is generating the one-line purpose summary per file. Everything else (imports, file paths, hotspot counting) is static analysis — zero token cost.

---

**Per file cost:**

```
Input to AI:
  file content          → ~300-800 tokens (avg TS/TSX file)
  prompt instruction    → ~50 tokens
  
Output from AI:
  one-line summary      → ~15-20 tokens

Per file total          → ~400-900 tokens
```

---

**Full codebase estimate:**

```
Small RN app   (~50 files)   → 50  × 600 avg = ~30,000 tokens
Medium RN app  (~150 files)  → 150 × 600 avg = ~90,000 tokens
Large RN app   (~300 files)  → 300 × 600 avg = ~180,000 tokens
```

---

**This sounds expensive but consider:**

```
neo init runs ONCE (or rarely)

Session savings per day:
  5 tasks × 3,000 tokens saved = 15,000 tokens/day

Break even:
  Small app  → recoups cost in ~2 days
  Medium app → recoups cost in ~6 days
  Large app  → recoups cost in ~12 days
```

After break even, every session is pure savings.

---

**How to make neo init cheaper:**

**Batch similar files**
```
Instead of: AI call per file
Do:         Send 10 files per AI call, get 10 summaries back

Cost reduction: ~60% (fewer prompt overhead tokens)
```

**Skip obvious files**
```
src/types/Feed.ts        → just extract type names, no AI needed
src/store/feedSlice.ts   → pattern is obvious, minimal AI needed
src/assets/*             → skip entirely
```

**Only AI-summarize ambiguous files**
```
Clear pattern   → static template  → 0 tokens
Unclear purpose → AI summary       → ~600 tokens

~40% of RN files have clear patterns (screens, slices, types)
Potential saving: 40% of total init cost
```

---

**Realistic neo init cost for a medium RN app:**

```
Without optimization:  ~90,000 tokens  (~$0.27 at Sonnet pricing)
With batching + skip:  ~35,000 tokens  (~$0.10)

One time cost. Never repeated unless full regeneration needed.
```

---

**The number that matters most:**

```
neo init cost     → one time ~35,000 tokens
Daily savings     → ~15,000 tokens/day
ROI               → positive after day 3
```

---

Want to design the batching strategy for the generator next? That's where you cut the init cost significantly.

chat_messages.text:
The user wants Neo to be language-agnostic. Let me think about how to design Neo to work across any codebase.Absolutely right. Good catch. Let's step back and think language-agnostic.

---

**The core insight:**

Every codebase regardless of language has the same fundamental concepts:

```
Files          → exist in every codebase
Dependencies   → file A uses file B (imports, includes, requires)
Purpose        → what does this file do
Patterns       → how things are done here
Entry points   → where execution starts
Hotspots       → most depended-upon files
```

These are universal. Language is irrelevant.

---

**What changes across languages:**

```
Language    Import syntax         Entry point
TypeScript  import x from 'y'    index.ts / App.tsx
Python      from y import x      main.py / __init__.py
Go          import "y"           main.go
Rust        use crate::y         main.rs / lib.rs
Java        import com.y.x       Main.java
Ruby        require 'y'          application.rb
```

Only the *parsing* layer changes. The Neo structure stays identical.

---

**Language-agnostic Neo architecture:**

```
[Parser Layer]          → language specific, pluggable
        ↓
[Core Neo Engine]       → language agnostic, always same
        ↓
[neo.md]                → identical structure regardless of language
```

Think of it like a compiler frontend/backend separation. You swap the frontend (parser), the backend (Neo engine) never changes.

---

**Revised neo.md format — fully language agnostic:**

```markdown
# NEO
generated: 2024-01-15 | files: 87 | language: typescript | version: 1

## STRUCTURE
src/auth/token.ts | JWT creation and validation | deps: config/env, lib/errors
src/models/User.py | User database model | deps: db/connection, types/user
main.go            | application entry, server setup | deps: router, config
lib/auth.rb        | session and auth logic | deps: models/user, utils/crypto

## CONVENTIONS
error-handling: [describe pattern]
api-calls: [describe pattern]
config/env: [where env vars live]
testing: [testing pattern]
entry: [main entry point]

## HOTSPOTS
src/utils/http.ts | 34 dependents | edit carefully
src/types/index.ts | 28 dependents | edit carefully

## ENTRY POINTS
main: [file]
config: [file]
routing: [file]
```

No TypeScript specific sections. No React Native specific sections. Pure universal concepts.

---

**The pluggable parser layer:**

```
neo init --lang typescript    → uses TS parser
neo init --lang python        → uses Python parser
neo init --lang go            → uses Go parser
neo init                      → auto-detects language
```

Each parser extracts the same three things:
```
1. file path
2. direct dependencies (what it imports)
3. reverse dependencies (what imports it)
```

That's all the parser needs to do. Purpose summary is handled by AI — completely language agnostic.

---

**Auto-detection strategy:**

```
scan root for:
  package.json      → javascript/typescript
  requirements.txt  → python
  go.mod            → go
  Cargo.toml        → rust
  pom.xml           → java
  Gemfile           → ruby
  mix.exs           → elixir
```

No user input needed in most cases.

---

**What this means for your build:**

```
Core Neo engine     → build once, never changes
Parser per language → community can contribute
AI summary layer    → already language agnostic (LLM understands all languages)
neo.md format       → identical always
```

This is actually a significant scope expansion — but in a good way. Neo becomes a tool the entire dev community can use, not just TypeScript developers.

---

**Revised build order:**

```
Phase 1 → build core engine + TypeScript parser (your RN codebase)
Phase 2 → validate token savings hypothesis
Phase 3 → open source parser interface, community adds languages
```

Start narrow, design wide. Does this architecture resonate?

chat_messages.text:
The user has three questions/decisions:
1. Rust - confirmed
2. Convention extraction: manual vs AI inference
3. neo update called by coding agent when finished - is this best approach?

Let me think through each carefully.Great choices. Let me address each one honestly.

---

**Rust — excellent choice:**
```
Fast parsing        → codebase scans in milliseconds
Low memory          → fits the "less memory" philosophy of Neo
Single binary       → easy distribution, no runtime dependencies
Cross platform      → works everywhere
```

Perfectly aligned with Neo's goals.

---

**Manual vs AI convention extraction:**

Honest answer — **AI inference wins.**

```
Manual extraction:
  developer writes conventions themselves
  → accurate but only as good as developer's awareness
  → developers often don't document what they do instinctively
  → misses implicit patterns

AI inference:
  scans 20-30 files, detects recurring patterns
  → catches things developer forgot to document
  → sees patterns across entire codebase objectively
  → "you use AppError everywhere" — developer might not realize this
```

Real example:
```
Developer thinks:  "we handle errors consistently"
AI sees:           "67% of files use AppError, 
                    23% use console.error, 
                    10% use custom handlers"
                   → surfaces inconsistency developer didn't know existed
```

AI inference is more accurate AND more valuable. Manual is just a CLAUDE.md by another name.

**One caveat:** AI inferred conventions should be shown to developer for confirmation before writing to Neo. Don't silently assume.

```
neo init detected these conventions:
  error-handling: AppError class (found in 34 files)
  api-calls: services/api/* pattern (found in 28 files)
  styling: StyleSheet.create (found in 41 files)

Confirm? (yes/edit/skip)
```

---

**Coding agent calling neo update when finished:**

This is good but has one gap.

```
Current proposal:
  agent finishes work → calls neo update → done

The gap:
  agent modifies 5 files in one task
  calls neo update once at the end
  → which files does it update?
  → does it remember all files it touched?
  → what if agent crashes mid-task?
```

**Better approach — two triggers:**

```
Trigger 1: Agent calls neo update explicitly
  → agent passes list of all files it modified
  → neo updates all of them atomically

neo update src/auth/token.ts src/api/user.ts src/types/User.ts
```

```
Trigger 2: git hook as safety net
  pre-commit hook detects changed files
  → runs neo update on exactly those files
  → catches anything agent forgot
```

```
Agent finishes    → neo update (primary)
git commit        → neo validate (safety net)
  if stale        → neo update automatically
  if in sync      → commit proceeds
```

This way:
```
Happy path:   agent updates Neo → git hook confirms → perfect
Agent misses: git hook catches it → Neo still accurate
Agent crashes: git hook saves it → no stale Neo ever
```

---

**Revised decision summary:**

```
Language:       Rust ✅
Conventions:    AI inferred + developer confirmation ✅
Neo update:     Agent triggered + git hook safety net ✅
```

---

Ready to start speccing the Rust project structure? We can define the crate layout, core modules, and parser interface before writing any code.

chat_messages.text: how does coding agent know, it has to look for neo file for looking up or search code base?

content.text: how does coding agent know, it has to look for neo file for looking up or search code base?

chat_messages.text:
This is a critical question. You've spotted a real gap.

---

**The problem:**

```
Neo exists in root
Coding agent starts new session
Agent doesn't know Neo exists
Agent starts grepping anyway
Neo is useless
```

Neo is only valuable if the agent **instinctively reaches for it first.**

---

**The answer: CLAUDE.md**

This is where CLAUDE.md and Neo work together. CLAUDE.md is the agent's instruction manual. Neo is the codebase map.

```markdown
# CLAUDE.md

## IMPORTANT — Before doing anything:
1. Read neo.md in project root
2. Use neo.md to locate relevant files
3. Never grep the codebase if neo.md has the answer
4. After making changes, run: neo update <files you changed>
```

One paragraph in CLAUDE.md. Agent reads it at session start. Agent knows Neo exists and knows to use it.

---

**The relationship between the two files:**

```
CLAUDE.md    →    HOW to work in this codebase (instructions)
neo.md       →    WHAT is in this codebase (map)

CLAUDE.md tells agent to read neo.md
neo.md tells agent where files are
```

They're complementary. Neither replaces the other.

---

**The instruction needs to be very explicit:**

```markdown
## Codebase Navigation — MANDATORY

A neo.md file exists in the project root.
It contains the full codebase map.

ALWAYS:
  read neo.md FIRST before any file search
  use neo.md to locate files directly
  run `neo update <filepath>` after every file change

NEVER:
  grep the codebase if neo.md can answer it
  skip updating neo.md after changes
```

Coding agents follow explicit instructions very reliably. Vague suggestions get ignored.

---

**But what about other agents? Not just Claude Code?**

CLAUDE.md only works for Claude Code. If Neo is language agnostic, it should also be agent agnostic.

Solution — a universal instruction file per agent:

```
CLAUDE.md       → Claude Code
.cursorrules    → Cursor
AGENTS.md       → OpenAI Codex, general agents
.github/copilot → GitHub Copilot
```

Neo ships with a generator for all of them:

```bash
neo init --agent claude     # writes CLAUDE.md instructions
neo init --agent cursor     # writes .cursorrules instructions
neo init --agent all        # writes all of them
```

Same Neo file. Different agent instruction files pointing to it.

---

**This also means neo init does three things:**

```
1. Scans codebase → generates neo.md
2. Detects which agents are in use → generates instruction files
3. Optionally installs git hook → safety net for neo update
```

Single command. Everything wired up. Agent knows about Neo from first session.

---

**The full flow after neo init:**

```
Developer runs neo init
  → neo.md created
  → CLAUDE.md updated with Neo instructions
  → git hook installed

Developer opens Claude Code
  → agent reads CLAUDE.md
  → agent reads neo.md
  → agent navigates directly to files
  → no grepping

Developer finishes task
  → agent runs neo update
  → git hook validates
  → everything stays in sync
```

---

**This is actually a key insight for Neo's value proposition:**

Neo isn't just a file format. It's a **system**:

```
neo.md          → the map
CLAUDE.md       → the instruction to use the map
neo update      → the maintenance mechanism
git hook        → the safety net
neo init        → the setup that wires all of it together
```

Remove any one piece and the system breaks down.

Does this complete the picture? Ready to start defining the Rust project structure?

```
neo.md       →    WHAT is in this codebase (map)

CLAUDE.md tells agent to read neo.md
neo.md tells agent where files are
```

They're complementary. Neither replaces the other.

---

**The instruction needs to be very explicit:**

```markdown
## Codebase Navigation — MANDATORY

A neo.md file exists in the project root.
It contains the full codebase map.

ALWAYS:
  read neo.md FIRST before any file search
  use neo.md to locate files directly
  run `neo update <filepath>` after every file change

NEVER:
  grep the codebase if neo.md can answer it
  skip updating neo.md after changes
```

Coding agents follow explicit instructions very reliably. Vague suggestions get ignored.

---

**But what about other agents? Not just Claude Code?**

CLAUDE.md only works for Claude Code. If Neo is language agnostic, it should also be agent agnostic.

Solution — a universal instruction file per agent:

```
CLAUDE.md       → Claude Code
.cursorrules    → Cursor
AGENTS.md       → OpenAI Codex, general agents
.github/copilot → GitHub Copilot
```

Neo ships with a generator for all of them:

```bash
neo init --agent claude     # writes CLAUDE.md instructions
neo init --agent cursor     # writes .cursorrules instructions
neo init --agent all        # writes all of them
```

Same Neo file. Different agent instruction files pointing to it.

---

**This also means neo init does three things:**

```
1. Scans codebase → generates neo.md
2. Detects which agents are in use → generates instruction files
3. Optionally installs git hook → safety net for neo update
```

Single command. Everything wired up. Agent knows about Neo from first session.

---

**The full flow after neo init:**

```
Developer runs neo init
  → neo.md created
  → CLAUDE.md updated with Neo instructions
  → git hook installed

Developer opens Claude Code
  → agent reads CLAUDE.md
  → agent reads neo.md
  → agent navigates directly to files
  → no grepping

Developer finishes task
  → agent runs neo update
  → git hook validates
  → everything stays in sync
```

---

**This is actually a key insight for Neo's value proposition:**

Neo isn't just a file format. It's a **system**:

```
neo.md          → the map
CLAUDE.md       → the instruction to use the map
neo update      → the maintenance mechanism
git hook        → the safety net
neo init        → the setup that wires all of it together
```

Remove any one piece and the system breaks down.

```rust
// src/main.rs

use clap::{Parser, Subcommand};

#[derive(Parser)]
#[command(name = "neo")]
#[command(about = "AI-native codebase map for coding agents")]
struct Cli {
    #[command(subcommand)]
    command: Commands,
}

#[derive(Subcommand)]
enum Commands {
    /// Scan codebase and generate neo.md
    Init {
        #[arg(long, default_value = "all")]
        agent: String,
    },
    /// Update neo.md for specific files
    Update {
        files: Vec<String>,
    },
    /// Check if neo.md is in sync with codebase
    Validate,
}

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() -> anyhow::Result<()> {
    let cli = Cli::parse();
    
    match cli.command {
        Commands::Init { agent } => {
            neo_core::cli::init::run(agent).await?
        }
        Commands::Update { files } => {
            neo_core::cli::update::run(files).await?
        }
        Commands::Validate => {
            neo_core::cli::validate::run().await?
        }
    }
    
    Ok(())
}
```

Good question to ask before writing code. Distribution strategy affects how you structure the build.

---

**Options:**

**1. cargo install (simplest)**
```bash
cargo install neo-cli
```
- Compiles from source on user's machine
- Requires Rust installed
- Not beginner friendly
- Good for Rust developers only

---

**2. Homebrew (Mac/Linux)**
```bash
brew install neo
```
- Most developers already have brew
- No Rust required
- Clean install/uninstall/upgrade
- Requires maintaining a Homebrew tap

---

**3. npm/npx (widest reach)**
```bash
npx neo-cli init
# or
npm install -g neo-cli
```
- Every developer has Node.js
- Zero friction for JS/TS developers
- Rust binary ships inside npm package
- Cursor, ESLint, Prettier all do this

---

**4. Shell script**
```bash
curl -fsSL https://get.neo.dev | sh
```
- One liner, no prerequisites
- Downloads prebuilt binary
- Works everywhere
- Security conscious devs distrust curl pipes

---

**5. Direct binary download**
```
github.com/neo/releases
  neo-macos-arm64
  neo-macos-x86
  neo-linux-x86
  neo-windows.exe
```
- Manual download, no package manager needed
- Always works as fallback

---

**Reality of adoption:**

```
Rust devs        → cargo install
Mac/Linux devs   → homebrew
JS/TS devs       → npm/npx       ← your primary audience
Windows devs     → binary download
CI/CD            → binary download or npm
```

Since Neo's primary audience is developers working with codebases (heavy JS/TS overlap), **npm is your widest reach.**

---

**Recommended strategy — ship all of them:**

```
Phase 1 → cargo install (zero effort, just publish to crates.io)
Phase 2 → npm/npx (wraps Rust binary, reaches JS devs)
Phase 3 → homebrew (after traction, worth maintaining)
Phase 4 → shell script (for CI/CD use cases)
```

Direct binary on GitHub releases comes free with every release via GitHub Actions — always have this regardless.

---

**How npm wrapping works for a Rust binary:**

```
neo-cli (npm package)
├── package.json
├── install.js        → downloads correct binary for platform
└── bin/
    └── neo.js        → thin wrapper that calls the binary
```

User runs `npx neo-cli init` → npm downloads binary for their platform → runs it. They never know it's Rust.

This is exactly how tools like **esbuild, swc, turbo** distribute Rust/Go binaries through npm.

---

**CI/CD usage:**

```yaml
# GitHub Actions
- name: Update Neo
  run: npx neo-cli validate
```

Zero setup in CI. Just works.

---

**My recommendation for MVP:**

```
Start:   cargo install    (publish to crates.io, zero effort)
Then:    GitHub releases  (binary downloads, comes free)
Then:    npm/npx          (unlocks JS/TS developer audience)
```

Three distribution channels cover 95% of your audience.

input.file_text:
[package]
name = "neo"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"
description = "AI-native codebase map for coding agents"
authors = ["Neo Contributors"]
license = "MIT"

# Produces the `neo` CLI binary
[[bin]]
name = "neo"
path = "src/main.rs"

# Core engine exposed as a library so it can be tested
# and potentially reused by other tools in the future
[lib]
name = "neo_core"
path = "src/lib.rs"

[dependencies]
# CLI argument parsing with derive macros
# e.g. `neo init --agent claude`
clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive"] }

# Recursive directory walking
# used by scanner to traverse codebase
walkdir = "2"

# Async runtime — needed for parallel AI API calls
# without this, summarizing 200 files would be sequential and slow
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full"] }

# HTTP client for calling AI APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI etc.)
reqwest = { version = "0.11", features = ["json"] }

# Serialization/deserialization for internal data structures
# and for reading/writing neo.md metadata
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1"

# Ergonomic error handling
# anyhow    → for application-level errors (CLI)
# thiserror → for library-level typed errors (core engine)
anyhow = "1"
thiserror = "1"

# Terminal UX
# indicatif → progress bars during scanning
# colored   → colored output for success/error messages
indicatif = "0.17"
colored = "2"

# Timestamps for neo.md metadata
chrono = { version = "0.4", features = ["serde"] }

# Regex for import extraction in parsers
regex = "1"

[dev-dependencies]
# Creates temporary directories for integration tests
# so tests never touch real filesystems
tempfile = "3"

The correct Rust pattern:

```
lib.rs   → owns all module declarations (types, core, parser, ai, agent)
main.rs  → uses the library, only adds `mod cli` (CLI-only code)
```

content.text:
Good catch. Here's what was wrong and what was fixed:

**The bug:**
```
Before:
  lib.rs   → declares: types, core, parser, ai, agent
  main.rs  → also declares: types, core, parser, ai, agent  ← duplicate!

Result: two separate module trees, types are technically incompatible
        between the binary and library
```

**The fix:**
```
After:
  lib.rs   → owns all shared modules (types, core, parser, ai, agent)
  main.rs  → mod cli (only CLI code) + use neo_core::*
  cli/*.rs → use neo_core:: instead of crate::
```

This is the standard Rust pattern for a crate that's both a binary and a library. `lib.rs` is the single source of truth for all shared code.

1	/// types.rs
     2	/// 
     3	/// Core data structures shared across all Neo modules.
     4	/// Every module imports from here — nothing else.
     5	/// 
     6	/// Design principle: keep types minimal and flat.
     7	/// Nested structures balloon neo.md size unnecessarily.
     8	
     9	use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
    10	
    11	/// Represents a single file entry in Neo.
    12	/// 
    13	/// This is the fundamental unit Neo tracks.
    14	/// One NeoFile = one file in the codebase.
    15	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    16	pub struct NeoFile {
    17	    /// Relative path from project root
    18	    /// e.g. "src/auth/token.ts"
    19	    pub path: String,
    20	
    21	    /// One-line AI-generated description of what this file does
    22	    /// e.g. "JWT creation, validation and refresh logic"
    23	    /// Kept to one line intentionally — every extra word costs tokens
    24	    pub purpose: String,
    25	
    26	    /// Files this file directly imports/requires/depends on
    27	    /// e.g. ["src/config/env.ts", "src/lib/errors.ts"]
    28	    /// Only direct deps, not transitive — keeps Neo lean
    29	    pub deps: Vec<String>,
    30	
    31	    /// Files that import THIS file (reverse dependency)
    32	    /// Computed by the graph builder, not the parser
    33	    /// High count = hotspot candidate
    34	    pub dependents: Vec<String>,
    35	
    36	    /// Number of files that depend on this file
    37	    /// Cached here so hotspot detection is O(1)
    38	    pub hotspot_score: u32,
    39	}
    40	
    41	/// Metadata about the Neo index itself
    42	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    43	pub struct NeoConfig {
    44	    /// Detected language of the codebase
    45	    /// e.g. "typescript", "python", "rust"
    46	    pub language: String,
    47	
    48	    /// ISO 8601 timestamp when Neo was last generated/updated
    49	    pub generated_at: String,
    50	
    51	    /// Total number of files tracked in this Neo index
    52	    pub file_count: u32,
    53	
    54	    /// Neo format version — increment when format changes
    55	    /// Allows future tooling to handle old neo.md files gracefully
    56	    pub version: u8,
    57	}
    58	
    59	/// A detected or manually specified coding convention
    60	/// 
    61	/// Examples:
    62	///   key: "error-handling"  value: "AppError class"    confidence: 0.91
    63	///   key: "api-calls"       value: "services/api/* only" confidence: 0.87
    64	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    65	pub struct NeoConvention {
    66	    /// Short label for the convention
    67	    /// e.g. "error-handling", "state-management", "api-calls"
    68	    pub key: String,
    69	
    70	    /// Human-readable description of the convention
    71	    /// e.g. "Always use AppError class from utils/errors.ts"
    72	    pub value: String,
    73	
    74	    /// 0.0 to 1.0 — how consistently this pattern appears in codebase
    75	    /// 0.9+ = very consistent, 0.5 = mixed, below 0.5 = not really a convention
    76	    pub confidence: f32,
    77	
    78	    /// Whether the developer has explicitly confirmed this convention
    79	    /// Unconfirmed conventions are shown to user during `neo init`
    80	    /// Only confirmed ones are written to neo.md
    81	    pub confirmed: bool,
    82	}
    83	
    84	/// A named entry point into the codebase
    85	/// 
    86	/// Examples:
    87	///   role: "app-entry"   path: "index.ts"
    88	///   role: "navigation"  path: "src/navigation/RootNavigator.tsx"
    89	///   role: "api-base"    path: "src/services/api/httpClient.ts"
    90	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    91	pub struct EntryPoint {
    92	    /// What role this entry point serves
    93	    /// e.g. "app-entry", "navigation", "db-config", "env-config"
    94	    pub role: String,
    95	
    96	    /// Relative path to the entry point file
    97	    pub path: String,
    98	}
    99	
   100	/// The complete Neo index for a codebase
   101	/// 
   102	/// This is what gets serialized to neo.md.
   103	/// All other types are components of this.
   104	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   105	pub struct Neo {
   106	    /// Index metadata (language, timestamp, version)
   107	    pub config: NeoConfig,
   108	
   109	    /// All tracked files with their purposes and dependencies
   110	    pub files: Vec<NeoFile>,
   111	
   112	    /// Detected coding conventions for this codebase
   113	    pub conventions: Vec<NeoConvention>,
   114	
   115	    /// Key entry points the coding agent should know about
   116	    pub entry_points: Vec<EntryPoint>,
   117	}
   118	
   119	impl Neo {
   120	    /// Creates a new empty Neo index
   121	    pub fn new(language: String) -> Self {
   122	        Neo {
   123	            config: NeoConfig {
   124	                language,
   125	                generated_at: chrono::Utc::now().to_rfc3339(),
   126	                file_count: 0,
   127	                version: 1,
   128	            },
   129	            files: Vec::new(),
   130	            conventions: Vec::new(),
   131	            entry_points: Vec::new(),
   132	        }
   133	    }
   134	
   135	    /// Returns files sorted by hotspot score descending
   136	    /// Used when writing the HOTSPOTS section of neo.md
   137	    pub fn hotspots(&self) -> Vec<&NeoFile> {
   138	        let mut sorted: Vec<&NeoFile> = self.files.iter()
   139	            .filter(|f| f.hotspot_score > 5) // only meaningful hotspots
   140	            .collect();
   141	        sorted.sort_by(|a, b| b.hotspot_score.cmp(&a.hotspot_score));
   142	        sorted
   143	    }
   144	
   145	    /// Finds a file entry by its path
   146	    pub fn find_file(&self, path: &str) -> Option<&NeoFile> {
   147	        self.files.iter().find(|f| f.path == path)
   148	    }
   149	
   150	    /// Updates or inserts a file entry
   151	    /// Used by `neo update` for incremental updates
   152	    pub fn upsert_file(&mut self, file: NeoFile) {
   153	        if let Some(existing) = self.files.iter_mut().find(|f| f.path == file.path) {
   154	            *existing = file;
   155	        } else {
   156	            self.files.push(file);
   157	        }
   158	        self.config.file_count = self.files.len() as u32;
   159	        self.config.generated_at = chrono::Utc::now().to_rfc3339();
   160	    }
   161	
   162	    /// Removes a file entry by path
   163	    /// Used when a file is deleted from the codebase
   164	    pub fn remove_file(&mut self, path: &str) {
   165	        self.files.retain(|f| f.path != path);
   166	        self.config.file_count = self.files.len() as u32;
   167	    }
   168	}
   169	
   170	/// Errors that can occur in the Neo core engine
   171	/// 
   172	/// Using thiserror for typed errors in the library layer.
   173	/// CLI layer converts these to anyhow errors for display.
   174	#[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
   175	pub enum NeoError {
   176	    #[error("neo.md not found. Run `neo init` first.")]
   177	    NotInitialized,
   178	
   179	    #[error("Failed to parse file: {path} — {reason}")]
   180	    ParseError { path: String, reason: String },
   181	
   182	    #[error("AI summarization failed: {0}")]
   183	    AiError(String),
   184	
   185	    #[error("IO error: {0}")]
   186	    IoError(#[from] std::io::Error),
   187	
   188	    #[error("Unsupported language: {0}")]
   189	    UnsupportedLanguage(String),
   190	}

# CLAUDE.md — Neo

## NEO — Codebase Map (READ FIRST)

A `neo.md` file will exist in the project root once `neo init` has been run.

**ALWAYS before searching the codebase:**
1. Read `neo.md` first
2. Use it to locate files directly
3. Only grep if `neo.md` doesn't have the answer

**ALWAYS after making file changes:**
Run: `neo update <space-separated list of files you changed>`
Example: `neo update src/core/graph.rs src/types.rs`

**NEVER:**
- Grep the codebase for something neo.md already maps
- Skip updating neo.md after changes
- Edit neo.md manually (it is auto-generated)

---

## Project Overview

Neo is a CLI tool written in Rust that generates a compact `neo.md` codebase map
for coding agents. The map lets agents navigate directly to files instead of
grepping the codebase — saving significant tokens per session.

---

## Build & Run

```bash
# Build
cargo build

# Run all tests
cargo test --lib

# Run a command
cargo run -- init --agent claude
cargo run -- update src/core/graph.rs
cargo run -- validate

# Release build
cargo build --release
# Binary at: target/release/neo
```

---

## Project Structure

```
src/
├── main.rs           CLI entry point — parses args, dispatches to cli/
├── lib.rs            Library entry point — declares all shared modules
├── types.rs          ALL shared types — import from here only
│
├── core/
│   ├── scanner.rs    Walks directory tree, returns indexable files
│   ├── graph.rs      Builds reverse dependency graph + hotspot scores
│   └── writer.rs     Reads/writes neo.md (parse + render)
│
├── parser/
│   ├── mod.rs        Parser trait definition — language-agnostic interface
│   ├── detector.rs   Auto-detects language from project root markers
│   ├── typescript.rs TypeScript/TSX import + export extraction
│   └── python.rs     Python import + export extraction
│
├── ai/
│   ├── summarizer.rs Batched AI file summarization (Anthropic API)
│   └── conventions.rs AI convention inference with confidence scores
│
├── agent/
│   └── claude.rs     Generates CLAUDE.md instructions (idempotent)
│
└── cli/
    ├── init.rs       `neo init` — full scan, orchestrates all modules
    ├── update.rs     `neo update` — incremental update for changed files
    └── validate.rs   `neo validate` — CI/CD sync checker
```

---

## Key Architecture Rules

**Module ownership:**
- `lib.rs` owns all shared modules (types, core, parser, ai, agent)
- `main.rs` owns only `cli/` — everything else comes from `neo_core::`
- `cli/` imports use `neo_core::` not `crate::` — they consume the library

**Types:**
- All shared types live in `types.rs` — never define types elsewhere
- `NeoError` uses `thiserror` (library layer)
- CLI layer uses `anyhow` for error display

**Parser trait:**
- Adding a language = implementing `Parser` trait in `parser/`
- Register the new parser in `parser/mod.rs → get_parser_for_file()`
- Core engine never knows what language it's dealing with

**AI calls:**
- Only `ai/summarizer.rs` and `ai/conventions.rs` make API calls
- Model: `claude-haiku-4-5-20251001` (fast + cheap for this task)
- Batch size: 10 files per API call
- API key: `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` env var — never hardcode

**neo.md format:**
- Pipe-delimited flat text — not JSON/YAML (avoids syntax token overhead)
- Sections: STRUCTURE, CONVENTIONS, HOTSPOTS, ENTRY POINTS
- Hotspot threshold: files with 3+ dependents
- Always sorted alphabetically so git diffs are stable

---

## Adding a New Language Parser

1. Create `src/parser/<language>.rs`
2. Implement the `Parser` trait:
   - `can_parse()` — check file extension
   - `extract_deps()` — parse import statements, return relative paths
   - `extract_exports()` — parse exported symbols
   - `extensions()` — return handled extensions
   - `language_name()` — return display name
3. Register in `src/parser/mod.rs → get_parser_for_file()`
4. Add detection marker in `src/parser/detector.rs → detect_language()`
5. Add entry point candidates in `src/cli/init.rs → detect_entry_points()`
6. Write tests in the same file under `#[cfg(test)]`

---

## Environment Variables

```
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY   Required for neo init and neo update (AI summarization)
```

---

## Testing

Tests live alongside the code in `#[cfg(test)]` blocks.

```bash
cargo test --lib                    # all library tests
cargo test --lib parser             # parser tests only
cargo test --lib core::writer       # specific module
```

Key test fixtures in tests/fixtures/ (coming soon):
- `typescript/` — sample TS project for integration tests
- `python/`     — sample Python project
- `rust/`       — sample Rust project

---

## What NOT to do

- Do not edit `neo.md` manually — it is always regenerated
- Do not add dependencies without checking Rust version compatibility
  (current environment: rustc 1.75 — some newer crates require 1.82+)
- Do not make AI API calls outside of `ai/` module
- Do not use `crate::` in `cli/` — use `neo_core::` instead
- Do not define types outside of `types.rs`

```bash
# Build
cargo build

# Run all tests
cargo test --lib

# Run a command
cargo run -- init --agent claude
cargo run -- update src/core/graph.rs
cargo run -- validate

# Release build
cargo build --release
# Binary at: target/release/neo
```

---

## Project Structure

```
src/
├── main.rs           CLI entry point — parses args, dispatches to cli/
├── lib.rs            Library entry point — declares all shared modules
├── types.rs          ALL shared types — import from here only
│
├── core/
│   ├── scanner.rs    Walks directory tree, returns indexable files
│   ├── graph.rs      Builds reverse dependency graph + hotspot scores
│   └── writer.rs     Reads/writes neo.md (parse + render)
│
├── parser/
│   ├── mod.rs        Parser trait definition — language-agnostic interface
│   ├── detector.rs   Auto-detects language from project root markers
│   ├── typescript.rs TypeScript/TSX import + export extraction
│   └── python.rs     Python import + export extraction
│
├── ai/
│   ├── summarizer.rs Batched AI file summarization (Anthropic API)
│   └── conventions.rs AI convention inference with confidence scores
│
├── agent/
│   └── claude.rs     Generates CLAUDE.md instructions (idempotent)
│
└── cli/
    ├── init.rs       `neo init` — full scan, orchestrates all modules
    ├── update.rs     `neo update` — incremental update for changed files
    └── validate.rs   `neo validate` — CI/CD sync checker
```

---

## Key Architecture Rules

**Module ownership:**
- `lib.rs` owns all shared modules (types, core, parser, ai, agent)
- `main.rs` owns only `cli/` — everything else comes from `neo_core::`
- `cli/` imports use `neo_core::` not `crate::` — they consume the library

**Types:**
- All shared types live in `types.rs` — never define types elsewhere
- `NeoError` uses `thiserror` (library layer)
- CLI layer uses `anyhow` for error display

**Parser trait:**
- Adding a language = implementing `Parser` trait in `parser/`
- Register the new parser in `parser/mod.rs → get_parser_for_file()`
- Core engine never knows what language it's dealing with

**AI calls:**
- Only `ai/summarizer.rs` and `ai/conventions.rs` make API calls
- Model: `claude-haiku-4-5-20251001` (fast + cheap for this task)
- Batch size: 10 files per API call
- API key: `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` env var — never hardcode

**neo.md format:**
- Pipe-delimited flat text — not JSON/YAML (avoids syntax token overhead)
- Sections: STRUCTURE, CONVENTIONS, HOTSPOTS, ENTRY POINTS
- Hotspot threshold: files with 3+ dependents
- Always sorted alphabetically so git diffs are stable

---

## Adding a New Language Parser

1. Create `src/parser/<language>.rs`
2. Implement the `Parser` trait:
   - `can_parse()` — check file extension
   - `extract_deps()` — parse import statements, return relative paths
   - `extract_exports()` — parse exported symbols
   - `extensions()` — return handled extensions
   - `language_name()` — return display name
3. Register in `src/parser/mod.rs → get_parser_for_file()`
4. Add detection marker in `src/parser/detector.rs → detect_language()`
5. Add entry point candidates in `src/cli/init.rs → detect_entry_points()`
6. Write tests in the same file under `#[cfg(test)]`

---

## Environment Variables

```
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY   Required for neo init and neo update (AI summarization)
```

---

## Testing

Tests live alongside the code in `#[cfg(test)]` blocks.

```bash
cargo test --lib                    # all library tests
cargo test --lib parser             # parser tests only
cargo test --lib core::writer       # specific module
```

Key test fixtures in tests/fixtures/ (coming soon):
- `typescript/` — sample TS project for integration tests
- `python/`     — sample Python project
- `rust/`       — sample Rust project

---

## What NOT to do

- Do not edit `neo.md` manually — it is always regenerated
- Do not add dependencies without checking Rust version compatibility
  (current environment: rustc 1.75 — some newer crates require 1.82+)
- Do not make AI API calls outside of `ai/` module
- Do not use `crate::` in `cli/` — use `neo_core::` instead
- Do not define types outside of `types.rs`
